# Configuring EC2 Auto Scaling with Target Tracking for 50% CPU Utilization

## Overview

Target tracking scaling works like a thermostat вҖ” you set a target metric value (50% CPU), and AWS automatically scales your fleet up or down to maintain that target.

---

## Step 1: Create a Launch Template

Before creating an Auto Scaling group, you need a launch template defining your instance configuration:

```bash
aws ec2 create-launch-template \
  --launch-template-name "my-app-template" \
  --launch-template-data '{
    "ImageId": "ami-0abcdef1234567890",
    "InstanceType": "t3.medium",
    "IamInstanceProfile": {
      "Arn": "arn:aws:iam::123456789012:instance-profile/MyInstanceProfile"
    },
    "SecurityGroupIds": ["sg-0123456789abcdef0"],
    "UserData": "IyEvYmluL2Jhc2gKZWNobyAiSGVsbG8gV29ybGQi"
  }'
```

> **Instance type matters:** Choose a type where 50% CPU is a meaningful operational threshold. A `t3.medium` (2 vCPUs) will behave differently than a `c5.2xlarge` (8 vCPUs) under the same workload.

---

## Step 2: Create the Auto Scaling Group

```bash
aws autoscaling create-auto-scaling-group \
  --auto-scaling-group-name "my-asg" \
  --launch-template "LaunchTemplateName=my-app-template,Version=\$Latest" \
  --min-size 2 \
  --max-size 10 \
  --desired-capacity 2 \
  --vpc-zone-identifier "subnet-abc123,subnet-def456" \
  --health-check-type EC2 \
  --health-check-grace-period 300
```

| Parameter | Recommendation | Reason |
|-----------|---------------|--------|
| `MinSize` | вүҘ 2 | Ensures availability during scale-in |
| `MaxSize` | Based on cost/load estimates | Hard ceiling on spend |
| `HealthCheckGracePeriod` | 300s | Allows instances to fully initialize before health checks begin |

---

## Step 3: Attach the Target Tracking Policy

This is the core configuration вҖ” a single API call creates and manages all necessary CloudWatch alarms automatically:

```bash
aws autoscaling put-scaling-policy \
  --auto-scaling-group-name "my-asg" \
  --policy-name "cpu-target-tracking-50" \
  --policy-type "TargetTrackingScaling" \
  --target-tracking-configuration '{
    "PredefinedMetricSpecification": {
      "PredefinedMetricType": "ASGAverageCPUUtilization"
    },
    "TargetValue": 50.0,
    "ScaleOutCooldown": 60,
    "ScaleInCooldown": 300,
    "DisableScaleIn": false
  }'
```

### Key Parameters Explained

| Parameter | Value | Purpose |
|-----------|-------|---------|
| `PredefinedMetricType` | `ASGAverageCPUUtilization` | Tracks average CPU across **all instances** in the group |
| `TargetValue` | `50.0` | The percentage AWS will try to maintain |
| `ScaleOutCooldown` | `60s` | Short вҖ” respond quickly to load spikes |
| `ScaleInCooldown` | `300s` | Longer вҖ” avoid premature instance termination |
| `DisableScaleIn` | `false` | Allow both scale-out and scale-in |

---

## Step 4: Required IAM Permissions

The Auto Scaling service needs permissions to manage CloudWatch alarms and describe EC2 resources:

```json
{
  "Version": "2012-10-17",
  "Statement": [
    {
      "Effect": "Allow",
      "Action": [
        "cloudwatch:PutMetricAlarm",
        "cloudwatch:DescribeAlarms",
        "cloudwatch:DeleteAlarms",
        "ec2:DescribeInstances",
        "ec2:TerminateInstances",
        "autoscaling:DescribeAutoScalingGroups",
        "autoscaling:SetDesiredCapacity",
        "autoscaling:TerminateInstanceInAutoScalingGroup"
      ],
      "Resource": "*"
    }
  ]
}
```

**Trust policy** allowing Auto Scaling to assume the role:

```json
{
  "Version": "2012-10-17",
  "Statement": [
    {
      "Effect": "Allow",
      "Principal": {
        "Service": "autoscaling.amazonaws.com"
      },
      "Action": "sts:AssumeRole"
    }
  ]
}
```

---

## How the Scaling Logic Works

```
в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
в”Ӯ              Target: 50% CPU Utilization                в”Ӯ
в”ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Ө
в”Ӯ  CPU > 50%   в”Ӯ  Scale OUT вҶ’ Add instances               в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ              в”Ӯ  (avg CPU drops as load is distributed)  в”Ӯ
в”ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”јв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Ө
в”Ӯ  CPU = 50%   в”Ӯ  No action вҶ’ Steady state                в”Ӯ
в”ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”јв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Ө
в”Ӯ  CPU < 50%   в”Ӯ  Scale IN вҶ’ Remove instances             в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ              в”Ӯ  (avg CPU rises as load concentrates)    в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
```

AWS automatically creates **two CloudWatch alarms** behind the scenes:
- A **scale-out alarm** (CPU > target + buffer)
- A **scale-in alarm** (CPU < target вҲ’ buffer)

You do **not** need to create these manually вҖ” they are managed by the policy lifecycle.

---

## Verification Commands

```bash
# Confirm the policy was created
aws autoscaling describe-policies \
  --auto-scaling-group-name "my-asg" \
  --policy-names "cpu-target-tracking-50"

# Monitor scaling activity
aws autoscaling describe-scaling-activities \
  --auto-scaling-group-name "my-asg"

# Check current group state
aws autoscaling describe-auto-scaling-groups \
  --auto-scaling-group-names "my-asg" \
  --query 'AutoScalingGroups[0].{Min:MinSize,Max:MaxSize,Desired:DesiredCapacity,Instances:Instances[*].InstanceId}'
```

---

## Important Caveats

> вҡ пёҸ **Do not manually edit the CloudWatch alarms** created by target tracking. AWS will recreate or modify them automatically, and manual changes will be overwritten or cause conflicts.

> вҡ пёҸ **T-series burstable instances** (t3, t2) use CPU credits, which can cause misleading utilization metrics. Consider using fixed-performance instances (m5, c5) for workloads where CPU tracking is critical.

> вҡ пёҸ **Cooldown periods** prevent thrashing but mean the group won't respond instantly to every metric change. Tune `ScaleOutCooldown` aggressively low for latency-sensitive applications.

# Cost-Optimized Image Processing Pipeline: Lambda + Step Functions + ECS Fargate + SQS

## Architecture Overview

```
[S3 Upload Event]
      в”Ӯ
      в–ј
[SQS Queue] в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв–ә [Dead Letter Queue (DLQ)]
      в”Ӯ                (failed messages)
      в–ј
[Lambda] в—„в”Җв”Җ AWSLambdaSQSQueueExecutionRole
  (fan-out / routing / metadata inspection)
      в”Ӯ
      в”ңв”Җв”Җ Small files в”Җв”Җв–ә [Lambda] (direct processing, cheap + fast)
      в”Ӯ
      в””в”Җв”Җ Large files в”Җв”Җв–ә [Step Functions State Machine]
                                в”Ӯ
                    в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”јв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
                    в–ј           в–ј           в–ј
               [Validate]  [Resize]   [Watermark]
               (Lambda)    (Fargate)  (Fargate)
                                в”Ӯ
                           [Store Metadata]
                                в”Ӯ
                           [Notify / Complete]
```

---

## Component Roles

### 1. SQS вҖ” Traffic Buffering

**Purpose:** Absorbs upload bursts without overwhelming downstream processors.

**Key mechanisms:**
- **Visibility timeout** вҖ” prevents duplicate processing while a message is being handled; should be set longer than `Lambda timeout + Fargate startup time`
- **Dead Letter Queue (DLQ)** вҖ” isolates messages that fail repeatedly, enabling partial failure containment without blocking the main queue
- **Batch size tuning** вҖ” controls how many image jobs Lambda picks up per invocation, balancing throughput vs. failure blast radius

**Graph-backed evidence:** `AWSLambdaSQSQueueExecutionRole` confirms Lambda has permissions to receive and delete SQS messages, establishing the LambdaвҶ”SQS integration pattern.

---

### 2. Lambda вҖ” Fan-Out, Routing, and Lightweight Work

**Purpose:** Consumes SQS messages, inspects image metadata, and routes work to the appropriate processor.

**Key mechanisms:**
- **Event source mapping** вҖ” Lambda polls SQS automatically; backed by `AWSLambdaSQSQueueExecutionRole`
- **Routing logic:**
  ```
  if file_size < threshold and estimated_duration < 15min:
      process directly in Lambda
  else:
      trigger Step Functions execution for ECS Fargate
  ```
- **Per-item failure reporting** вҖ” Lambda can report individual batch item failures back to SQS, so only failed messages are requeued rather than the entire batch
- **Concurrency scaling** вҖ” Lambda scales horizontally per `aws.lambda.constraints`, handling burst traffic without pre-provisioning

**Cost lever:** Lambda's per-100ms billing makes it extremely cheap for short-lived routing and metadata extraction work.

**Graph-backed evidence:** `aws.lambda.constraints` / `aws.lambda.limits` define hard resource ceilings (15-min timeout, 10GB memory) that inform the routing threshold decision. `aws.lambda.vpc` is relevant if processing requires access to private endpoints.

---

### 3. Step Functions вҖ” Orchestration and Partial Failure Recovery

**Purpose:** Defines the image processing workflow as a state machine, handling sequencing, retries, and failure isolation per image job.

**Key mechanisms:**

**State machine structure:**
```
[Validate Image]
      в”Ӯ (success)
      в–ј
[Extract Metadata] (Lambda вҖ” fast, cheap)
      в”Ӯ
      в–ј
[Resize / Transform] (ECS Fargate Task вҖ” heavy compute)
      в”Ӯ
      в–ј
[Apply Watermark]   (ECS Fargate Task)
      в”Ӯ
      в–ј
[Store Results + Notify]
```

**Partial failure handling per state:**
```json
{
  "Retry": [{ "ErrorEquals": ["States.TaskFailed"],
              "IntervalSeconds": 2,
              "MaxAttempts": 3,
              "BackoffRate": 2 }],
  "Catch": [{ "ErrorEquals": ["States.ALL"],
              "Next": "HandleFailure" }]
}
```

- Each execution is **independent** вҖ” one image failing does not affect others
- `.sync:2` integration pattern allows Step Functions to **wait for Fargate Tasks** to complete without holding a Lambda execution open (avoids Lambda timeout issues on long-running jobs)
- **Express Workflows** are cheaper for high-volume, short-duration pipelines vs. Standard Workflows

*Note: Step Functions is not directly represented in the knowledge graph but its integration with Lambda and ECS Fargate is a well-established AWS architectural pattern.*

---

### 4. ECS Fargate вҖ” Heavy Image Processing

**Purpose:** Runs CPU/memory-intensive image transformations in isolated containers without managing EC2 infrastructure.

**Key mechanisms:**
- **Task isolation** вҖ” each image processing job runs as a separate Fargate Task; one task failure does not cascade to others (`aws.ecs.fargate.task` вҖ” isolated kernel environment)
- **No idle cost** вҖ” tasks spin up on demand and terminate immediately after processing; no EC2 instances sitting idle between bursts
- **Right-sized task definitions** вҖ” `aws.ecs.task_definition` allows precise vCPU/memory allocation per workload type:

| Workload | vCPU | Memory | Approx. Cost/Task |
|----------|------|--------|-------------------|
| Small image resize | 0.5 | 1 GB | ~$0.01 |
| Large RAW processing | 2.0 | 8 GB | ~$0.08 |
| Batch watermarking | 1.0 | 4 GB | ~$0.04 |

- **Fargate Spot** вҖ” use for non-urgent batch queues to achieve ~70% cost reduction
- **ECS Service auto scaling** вҖ” `aws.ecs.service` integrates with CloudWatch metrics to scale task count in response to queue depth

**Graph-backed evidence:**
- `aws.ecs.fargate.billing` / `aws.ecs.fargate.pricing` вҖ” per-vCPU and per-GB-memory per-second billing
- `aws.fargate` вҖ” serverless compute, no EC2 management overhead
- `aws.ecs.task_definition` вҖ” defines container image, CPU, memory, IAM role
- `aws.ecs.service` вҖ” manages desired task count, load balancer integration, auto scaling
- `aws.ecs.task.container` вҖ” individual container within a task definition

---

## Handling the Three Key Challenges

### Challenge 1: Bursty Traffic

```
Burst of uploads
    в”Ӯ
    в–ј
SQS absorbs the burst (no messages dropped, no downstream overwhelm)
    в”Ӯ
    в–ј
Lambda scales concurrently (bounded by aws.lambda.constraints account limits)
    в”Ӯ
    в–ј
Step Functions executions scale independently (no shared state bottleneck)
    в”Ӯ
    в–ј
Fargate Tasks scale horizontally via ECS Service auto scaling
    в”Ӯ
    в–ј
Result: Smooth processing at any traffic level, no pre-provisioned capacity
```

**Key insight:** SQS acts as the shock absorber. Without it, a burst would either drop requests or require over-provisioned always-on compute.

---

### Challenge 2: Large Files

```
Lambda inspects S3 object size from event metadata
    в”Ӯ
    в”ңв”Җв”Җ File < threshold вҶ’ Lambda processes directly (fast, cheap)
    в”Ӯ
    в””в”Җв”Җ File > threshold вҶ’ Step Functions вҶ’ ECS Fargate Task
                                в”Ӯ
                                в–ј
                    Fargate streams directly from S3
                    (no Lambda 10GB memory constraint)
                    (no Lambda 15-min timeout constraint)
                    Task Definition allocates appropriate
                    vCPU/memory (aws.ecs.task_definition)
```

**Key insight:** Lambda's hard limits (`aws.lambda.limits`) make it unsuitable for large file processing. Fargate's configurable task definitions handle arbitrarily large workloads.

---

### Challenge 3: Partial Failures

Failures are isolated at **four independent layers:**

| Layer | Mechanism | Scope of Isolation |
|-------|-----------|-------------------|
| **SQS DLQ** | Messages failing N times routed to DLQ | Per-message |
| **Lambda batch** | Per-item failure reporting to SQS | Per-image within a batch |
| **Step Functions** | Per-state `Catch`/`Retry` blocks | Per-processing-step per image |
| **Fargate Task** | Isolated kernel environment (`aws.ecs.fargate.task`) | Per-container execution |

**Result:** A single corrupted image or transient API failure affects only that specific job. All other images continue processing normally.

---

## Cost Optimization Summary

| Component | Primary Cost Lever | Implementation |
|-----------|-------------------|----------------|
| **SQS** | Near-zero cost at scale | Prevents over-provisioning downstream; pay per request |
| **Lambda** | Per-100ms billing | Route only fast/small work here; avoid long-running tasks |
| **Step Functions** | Express Workflows | Use for high-volume short pipelines vs. Standard Workflows |
| **Fargate (on-demand)** | Per-second billing (`aws.ecs.fargate.billing`) | Tasks terminate immediately after job completion |
| **Fargate Spot** | ~70% discount vs. on-demand | Apply to non-urgent batch processing queues |
| **Task right-sizing** | Match vCPU/memory to workload | Use `aws.ecs.task_definition` to avoid over-allocation |

---

- Fargate billing model via `aws.ecs.fargate.billing` / `aws.ecs.fargate.pricing`

# EKS Cluster Autoscaler with Managed Node Groups

> **Note:** This answer is based on general AWS/Kubernetes expertise, as the provided knowledge base does not contain EKS-specific documentation. Verify details against current AWS EKS documentation.

---

## Overview

The Cluster Autoscaler (CA) automatically adjusts the number of worker nodes in your EKS cluster when pods fail to schedule due to insufficient resources or when nodes are underutilized.

---

## Prerequisites

### 1. Managed Node Group Requirements
```bash
# Node groups must have proper tags for CA discovery
Key:   k8s.io/cluster-autoscaler/<cluster-name>
Value: owned

Key:   k8s.io/cluster-autoscaler/enabled
Value: true
```

---

## Step 1: IAM Configuration

### Create IAM Policy for Cluster Autoscaler
```json
{
  "Version": "2012-10-17",
  "Statement": [
    {
      "Effect": "Allow",
      "Action": [
        "autoscaling:DescribeAutoScalingGroups",
        "autoscaling:DescribeAutoScalingInstances",
        "autoscaling:DescribeLaunchConfigurations",
        "autoscaling:DescribeScalingActivities",
        "autoscaling:DescribeTags",
        "autoscaling:SetDesiredCapacity",
        "autoscaling:TerminateInstanceInAutoScalingGroup",
        "ec2:DescribeImages",
        "ec2:DescribeInstanceTypes",
        "ec2:DescribeLaunchTemplateVersions",
        "ec2:GetInstanceTypesFromInstanceRequirements",
        "eks:DescribeNodegroup"
      ],
      "Resource": "*"
    }
  ]
}
```

### Attach Policy via IRSA (IAM Roles for Service Accounts)
```bash
# Create IAM OIDC provider for your cluster
eksctl utils associate-iam-oidc-provider \
  --cluster <cluster-name> \
  --region <region> \
  --approve

# Create service account with IAM role
eksctl create iamserviceaccount \
  --cluster=<cluster-name> \
  --namespace=kube-system \
  --name=cluster-autoscaler \
  --attach-policy-arn=arn:aws:iam::<account-id>:policy/ClusterAutoscalerPolicy \
  --override-existing-serviceaccounts \
  --region <region> \
  --approve
```

---

## Step 2: Create Managed Node Group

```bash
eksctl create nodegroup \
  --cluster <cluster-name> \
  --region <region> \
  --name managed-ng-1 \
  --node-type m5.large \
  --nodes 2 \
  --nodes-min 1 \
  --nodes-max 10 \
  --managed
```

Or via eksctl config file:
```yaml
# cluster-config.yaml
apiVersion: eksctl.io/v1alpha5
kind: ClusterConfig

metadata:
  name: my-cluster
  region: us-east-1

managedNodeGroups:
  - name: managed-ng-1
    instanceType: m5.large
    minSize: 1
    maxSize: 10
    desiredCapacity: 2
    labels:
      role: worker
    tags:
      k8s.io/cluster-autoscaler/enabled: "true"
      k8s.io/cluster-autoscaler/my-cluster: "owned"
    iam:
      withAddonPolicies:
        autoScaler: true
```

---

## Step 3: Deploy Cluster Autoscaler

```yaml
# cluster-autoscaler-deployment.yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: cluster-autoscaler
  namespace: kube-system
  labels:
    app: cluster-autoscaler
spec:
  replicas: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: cluster-autoscaler
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: cluster-autoscaler
      annotations:
        cluster-autoscaler.kubernetes.io/safe-to-evict: "false"
    spec:
      serviceAccountName: cluster-autoscaler
      containers:
        - name: cluster-autoscaler
          image: registry.k8s.io/autoscaling/cluster-autoscaler:v1.28.0
          command:
            - ./cluster-autoscaler
            - --v=4
            - --stderrthreshold=info
            - --cloud-provider=aws
            - --skip-nodes-with-local-storage=false
            - --expander=least-waste
            - --node-group-auto-discovery=asg:tag=k8s.io/cluster-autoscaler/enabled,k8s.io/cluster-autoscaler/<cluster-name>
            - --balance-similar-node-groups
            - --skip-nodes-with-system-pods=false
          env:
            - name: AWS_REGION
              value: us-east-1
          resources:
            limits:
              cpu: 100m
              memory: 600Mi
            requests:
              cpu: 100m
              memory: 600Mi
          volumeMounts:
            - name: ssl-certs
              mountPath: /etc/ssl/certs/ca-certificates.crt
              readOnly: true
      volumes:
        - name: ssl-certs
          hostPath:
            path: /etc/ssl/certs/ca-bundle.crt
```

```bash
kubectl apply -f cluster-autoscaler-deployment.yaml
```

---

## Step 4: Key Configuration Flags

| Flag | Description | Recommended Value |
|------|-------------|-------------------|
| `--expander` | Node group selection strategy | `least-waste` or `priority` |
| `--scale-down-delay-after-add` | Wait time before scale-down after scale-up | `10m` |
| `--scale-down-unneeded-time` | Time node must be unneeded before removal | `10m` |
| `--scale-down-utilization-threshold` | CPU/memory utilization threshold | `0.5` |
| `--balance-similar-node-groups` | Balance nodes across similar groups | `true` |
| `--skip-nodes-with-system-pods` | Prevent scale-down of system pod nodes | `false` |

---

## Step 5: Verify Operation

```bash
# Check CA logs
kubectl logs -f deployment/cluster-autoscaler \
  -n kube-system | grep -i "scale"

# Check CA status
kubectl get configmap cluster-autoscaler-status \
  -n kube-system -o yaml

# Watch node scaling
kubectl get nodes -w
```

---

## Important Notes

- **Version matching**: CA minor version must match your Kubernetes cluster minor version (e.g., CA `v1.28.x` for K8s `1.28`)
- **IRSA over node IAM roles**: Always prefer IRSA for security over attaching policies to node instance roles
- **Multiple node groups**: Use `--balance-similar-node-groups` when running multiple node groups with similar instance types for high availability across AZs

## Component 1: EKS as the Orchestration Foundation

EKS manages the Kubernetes control plane and provides the scheduling substrate that all other components depend on.

### Mixed-Architecture Node Registration

EKS nodes self-register with architecture labels that Kubernetes uses for scheduling decisions:

```yaml
# Automatically applied node labels
kubernetes.io/arch: arm64        # Graviton nodes
kubernetes.io/arch: amd64        # x86_64 nodes
karpenter.sh/capacity-type: spot # Injected by Karpenter
```

### Critical Prerequisite: Multi-Architecture Container Images

EKS itself does not solve the image compatibility problem. Workloads must provide OCI manifest lists:

```bash
# Build and push multi-arch manifest
docker buildx build \
  --platform linux/amd64,linux/arm64 \
  --tag 123456789.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/myapp:latest \
  --push .
```

**If images are single-arch only**, pods must use node selectors to prevent mis-scheduling:

```yaml
# Hard constraint for x86-only images
spec:
  nodeSelector:
    kubernetes.io/arch: amd64
```

---

## Component 2: Karpenter's Role in Cost Minimization

Karpenter replaces the Cluster Autoscaler with a fundamentally different provisioning model: it provisions nodes **reactively to pod requirements** rather than scaling predefined node groups.

### NodePool Configuration for Mixed Architecture + Spot

```yaml
apiVersion: karpenter.sh/v1beta1
kind: NodePool
metadata:
  name: cost-optimized-mixed-arch
spec:
  template:
    spec:
      requirements:
        # Architecture: prefer arm64 (Graviton), allow amd64
        - key: kubernetes.io/arch
          operator: In
          values: ["arm64", "amd64"]
        
        # Capacity: Spot first, On-Demand as fallback
        - key: karpenter.sh/capacity-type
          operator: In
          values: ["spot", "on-demand"]
        
        # Instance families: diversify for Spot availability
        # Graviton families: m7g, c7g, r7g, m6g, c6g
        # x86 families: m7i, c7i, m6i, c6i
        - key: karpenter.k8s.aws/instance-family
          operator: In
          values: ["m7g", "c7g", "m6g", "c6g", "m7i", "c7i", "m6i"]
        
        # Minimum size to avoid excessive overhead ratio
        - key: karpenter.k8s.aws/instance-size
          operator: NotIn
          values: ["nano", "micro", "small"]
      
      nodeClassRef:
        apiVersion: karpenter.k8s.aws/v1beta1
        kind: EC2NodeClass
        name: default
  
  # Consolidation: actively reclaim underutilized nodes
  disruption:
    consolidationPolicy: WhenUnderutilized
    consolidateAfter: 30s
    # Protect against over-aggressive consolidation
    budgets:
      - nodes: "20%"  # Never consolidate more than 20% simultaneously
  
  # Cost guardrail
  limits:
    cpu: "1000"
    memory: 4000Gi
```

### How Karpenter Selects the Cheapest Compatible Node

```
Pending Pod arrives
        в”Ӯ
        в–ј
Karpenter reads pod requirements:
  - Resource requests (CPU/memory)
  - Node selectors / affinity rules
  - Tolerations
  - Topology spread constraints
        в”Ӯ
        в–ј
Generates candidate instance types:
  - Filters by arch compatibility
  - Filters by resource fit
  - Ranks by: Spot price Г— availability score
        в”Ӯ
        в–ј
Selects: smallest Graviton Spot instance that fits
  Example: c7g.medium for a 0.4 CPU / 512Mi pod
        в”Ӯ
        в–ј
Provisions node via EC2 Fleet API (diversified)
```

**Key cost behaviors:**
| Behavior | Mechanism | Cost Impact |
|----------|-----------|-------------|
| Bin-packing | Selects smallest fitting instance | Eliminates wasted capacity |
| Consolidation | Replaces N underutilized nodes with fewer fuller ones | Continuous right-sizing |
| Spot-first | `capacity-type` ordering | 60-90% discount vs On-Demand |
| Graviton preference | arch affinity weighting | Additional ~20% vs x86 Spot |

---

## Component 3: Graviton Instance Strategy

Graviton (arm64) instances provide a compounding cost advantage when combined with Spot pricing.

### Cost Stack Example

```
x86_64 On-Demand m6i.xlarge:    $0.192/hr  (baseline)
x86_64 Spot m6i.xlarge:         ~$0.058/hr (~70% discount)
arm64 On-Demand m6g.xlarge:     $0.154/hr  (~20% cheaper than x86 OD)
arm64 Spot m6g.xlarge:          ~$0.046/hr (~76% total discount vs x86 OD)
```

### Workload Affinity: Preferring Graviton Without Hard-Requiring It

```yaml
# Prefer Graviton, but allow x86 if arm64 unavailable
spec:
  affinity:
    nodeAffinity:
      preferredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution:
        - weight: 80
          preference:
            matchExpressions:
              - key: kubernetes.io/arch
                operator: In
                values: ["arm64"]
      # Hard requirement only if image is arm64-only
      # requiredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution: ...
```

### Graviton Instance Family Selection Guide

```
Compute-optimized workloads:  c7g, c6g (Graviton3/2)
General purpose:              m7g, m6g
Memory-optimized:             r7g, r6g
Burstable (dev/test):         t4g
```

---

## Component 4: Spot Interruption Handling

This is the **reliability layer** that makes Spot viable for production workloads. Without it, Spot interruptions cause pod disruption and potential data loss.

### The Interruption Signal Timeline

```
T-120s: EC2 sends Spot interruption notice (2-minute warning)
        в””в”ҖвҶ’ EventBridge event: aws.ec2 / EC2 Spot Instance Interruption Warning

T-120s: EC2 may also send Rebalance Recommendation (earlier, softer signal)
        в””в”ҖвҶ’ EventBridge event: aws.ec2 / EC2 Instance Rebalance Recommendation

T-0:    Instance terminated by EC2
```

### Karpenter's Native Interruption Handling

Karpenter (v0.19+) handles interruptions natively without requiring the separate AWS Node Termination Handler:

```
EventBridge Rule вҶ’ SQS Queue вҶ’ Karpenter controller
                                      в”Ӯ
                                      в–ј
                              Receives interruption notice
                                      в”Ӯ
                                      в–ј
                              Cordons node immediately
                              (prevents new pod scheduling)
                                      в”Ӯ
                                      в–ј
                              Begins provisioning replacement node
                              (proactively, before drain completes)
                                      в”Ӯ
                                      в–ј
                              Drains node
                              (respects PodDisruptionBudgets)
                                      в”Ӯ
                                      в–ј
                              Pods reschedule onto new node
```

### Required EventBridge + SQS Configuration

```json
// EventBridge rule targeting SQS queue for Karpenter
{
  "source": ["aws.ec2"],
  "detail-type": [
    "EC2 Spot Instance Interruption Warning",
    "EC2 Instance Rebalance Recommendation",
    "EC2 Instance State-change Notification"
  ]
}
```

### Pod-Level Resilience Requirements

Spot interruption handling at the infrastructure level is **necessary but not sufficient**. Pods must also be resilient:

```yaml
# 1. PodDisruptionBudget: limit simultaneous evictions
apiVersion: policy/v1
kind: PodDisruptionBudget
metadata:
  name: myapp-pdb
spec:
  minAvailable: 2        # or maxUnavailable: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: myapp

---
# 2. Graceful shutdown: give pods time to finish in-flight work
spec:
  terminationGracePeriodSeconds: 60   # tune to workload
  containers:
    - name: myapp
      lifecycle:
        preStop:
          exec:
            command: ["/bin/sh", "-c", "sleep 5"]  # drain connections

---
# 3. Topology spread: avoid all pods on same node
spec:
  topologySpreadConstraints:
    - maxSkew: 1
      topologyKey: kubernetes.io/hostname
      whenUnsatisfiable: DoNotSchedule
      labelSelector:
        matchLabels:
          app: myapp
```

---

## Workload Classification Matrix

Not all workloads should use the same configuration:

| Workload Type | Arch | Capacity Type | Rationale |
|---------------|------|---------------|-----------|
| Stateless web services | arm64 preferred | Spot | Multi-arch images easy; interruption-tolerant |
| Batch/ML training | arm64 or amd64 | Spot with checkpointing | Long-running but resumable |
| Stateful databases | amd64 | On-Demand | Interruption risk too high |
| CI/CD runners | arm64 | Spot | Short-lived; interruption = retry |
| System components (CoreDNS, etc.) | amd64 | On-Demand | Cluster stability critical |

---

## Key Limitations and Risks

1. **Image compatibility is a hard gate**: Graviton savings are zero if your images aren't multi-arch. Audit your image registry before enabling arm64 nodes.

2. **Spot pool exhaustion**: In capacity-constrained regions/AZs, all Spot pools may be interrupted simultaneously. Mitigation: spread across 3+ AZs and 5+ instance families.

3. **Karpenter consolidation vs. PDB conflicts**: Aggressive consolidation settings can conflict with strict PDBs, causing consolidation to stall. Monitor `karpenter_disruption_consolidation_timeouts_total`.

4. **Graviton performance parity is workload-dependent**: Some workloads (certain JVM configurations, x86-optimized native libraries) may perform worse on arm64, negating cost savings.

5. **The knowledge graph provided does not document these components**: Validate all configuration details against current [Karpenter documentation](https://karpenter.sh/docs/) and [AWS EKS best practices](https://aws.github.io/aws-eks-best-practices/).

# IAM Roles, VPC Security Groups, and EC2 Instance Profiles: Secure EC2 Access to S3 and RDS

## Overview of the Three-Layer Security Model

Secure EC2 access to S3 and RDS relies on **two complementary control planes** working in concert:

| Control Plane | Mechanisms | Answers the Question |
|---|---|---|
| **Identity & Authorization** | IAM Role + Instance Profile + Policy | *Who is allowed to call which AWS APIs?* |
| **Network Access Control** | VPC Security Groups + VPC Endpoints | *Which network paths are permitted?* |

Neither layer alone is sufficient вҖ” IAM without network controls leaves traffic exposed to the internet, and network controls without IAM leave API access ungoverned.

---

## Layer 1: EC2 Instance Profiles (Identity Binding)

### What an Instance Profile Does

An **Instance Profile** is the binding mechanism that attaches an IAM Role to an EC2 instance. It solves a fundamental problem: how does an EC2 instance prove its identity to AWS services without hardcoded credentials?

The answer is the **Instance Metadata Service (IMDS)**:

```
EC2 Instance
    в”Ӯ
    в”ңв”Җв”Җ Application calls: http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/iam/security-credentials/
    в”Ӯ
    в””в”Җв”Җ IMDS returns: { AccessKeyId, SecretAccessKey, SessionToken, Expiration }
                      (Temporary, auto-rotating credentials вҖ” no hardcoding needed)
```

### Key Properties

- **One instance profile per EC2 instance** вҖ” but the profile can contain one IAM role
- **Credentials rotate automatically** вҖ” typically every 6 hours, transparent to the application
- **Least-privilege scoping** вҖ” the attached role defines the exact permission boundary
- **Permission Boundaries** (confirmed in graph as `aws.iam.roles.constraints`) can further constrain what the role is allowed to do, even if the policy would otherwise permit more

### Why This Matters for S3 and RDS

Without an instance profile, your application would need to store AWS credentials as environment variables or config files вҖ” a significant security risk. With an instance profile, the AWS SDK automatically retrieves and refreshes credentials from IMDS, and those credentials are scoped only to what the attached IAM role permits.

---

## Layer 2: IAM Roles and Policies (Authorization)

### Role Structure

```
Instance Profile
    в””в”Җв”Җ IAM Role  (aws.iam.roles вҖ” "defines permissions for AWS services and resources")
            в””в”Җв”Җ IAM Policy  (aws.iam.policy вҖ” "allow or deny access to AWS resources")
                    в”ңв”Җв”Җ S3 Permissions
                    в””в”Җв”Җ RDS Permissions
```

### Least-Privilege Policy Design

**For S3 access**, scope permissions to specific buckets and operations:

```json
{
  "Version": "2012-10-17",
  "Statement": [
    {
      "Effect": "Allow",
      "Action": [
        "s3:GetObject",
        "s3:PutObject"
      ],
      "Resource": "arn:aws:s3:::my-specific-bucket/*"
    },
    {
      "Effect": "Allow",
      "Action": "s3:ListBucket",
      "Resource": "arn:aws:s3:::my-specific-bucket"
    }
  ]
}
```

**For RDS access** using IAM database authentication (eliminates database passwords):

```json
{
  "Statement": [
    {
      "Effect": "Allow",
      "Action": "rds-db:connect",
      "Resource": "arn:aws:rds-db:us-east-1:123456789:dbuser:db-ABCDEFG/my_db_user"
    }
  ]
}
```

### IAM Role Constraints

The graph confirms `aws.iam.roles.constraints` covers *"session duration and permission boundaries"* вҖ” use these to:
- **Limit session duration** вҖ” reduce the window of exposure if credentials are somehow leaked
- **Apply permission boundaries** вҖ” set a maximum permissions ceiling even if policies are later expanded

---

## Layer 3: VPC Security Groups (Network Control)

### Security Groups as Stateful Firewalls

Security groups (confirmed in graph as `aws.ecs.networking.security_group`, with the same mechanism applying to EC2) control **inbound and outbound traffic at the network interface level**. They are stateful вҖ” return traffic for allowed outbound connections is automatically permitted.

### The Critical Design Pattern: Reference Security Groups by ID, Not IP

For RDS access, the most secure pattern is to reference the **EC2 security group ID** in the RDS security group's inbound rules, rather than using IP ranges:

```
EC2 Security Group (sg-ec2-app)          RDS Security Group (sg-rds-database)
в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ          в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
в”Ӯ Outbound Rules:             в”Ӯ          в”Ӯ Inbound Rules:                       в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  Port 443  вҶ’ S3 VPC         в”Ӯ          в”Ӯ  Port 5432 (PostgreSQL)              в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ            Endpoint         в”Ӯ          в”Ӯ  Source: sg-ec2-app  вҶҗ (SG ID ref)   в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  Port 5432 вҶ’ sg-rds-        в”Ӯ          в”Ӯ                                      в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ            database         в”Ӯ          в”Ӯ  All other inbound: DENY             в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  All other: DENY            в”Ӯ          в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
```

**Why reference SG IDs instead of IPs?**
- EC2 instances can have dynamic IPs
- SG-to-SG rules automatically track all instances in the referenced group
- No rule updates needed when instances scale or are replaced

### VPC Endpoints: Keeping Traffic Off the Internet

The graph confirms `aws.vpc.endpoints` provide *"private connections that allow VPC resources to access specific AWS services without requiring internet access."*

For S3, use a **Gateway VPC Endpoint** (free, route-table based):

```
EC2 Instance
    в”Ӯ
    в”Ӯ  (Traffic stays entirely within AWS network)
    в”Ӯ
    в–ј
S3 Gateway VPC Endpoint в”Җв”ҖвҶ’ S3 Bucket
    (No internet gateway, no NAT gateway required)
```

For RDS, since it runs within your VPC already, traffic stays private as long as both EC2 and RDS are in the same VPC (or connected VPCs) with appropriate security group rules.

---

## How All Three Layers Work Together: End-to-End Flow

```
в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
в”Ӯ                        EC2 Instance                              в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ                                                                  в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  1. App starts вҶ’ SDK queries IMDS for credentials               в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ     (Instance Profile вҶ’ IAM Role вҶ’ Temp Credentials)            в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ                                                                  в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  2. App calls s3:GetObject                                       в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ     вҶ’ IAM Policy checked: вң“ Allowed for this bucket             в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ     вҶ’ Network: Outbound 443 allowed by Security Group           в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ     вҶ’ Traffic routes via VPC Endpoint (no internet)             в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ                                                                  в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  3. App connects to RDS                                          в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ     вҶ’ IAM Policy checked: вң“ rds-db:connect allowed              в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ     вҶ’ Network: Outbound 5432 allowed to RDS Security Group      в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ     вҶ’ RDS Security Group: Inbound from EC2 SG вң“                 в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ     вҶ’ IAM auth token used instead of password                   в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
```

### The Two-Factor Check for Every Request

Every access attempt must pass **both** checks independently:

```
Request to S3 or RDS
        в”Ӯ
        в”ңв”Җв”ҖвҶ’ IAM Check (Identity Plane)
        в”Ӯ         Is this role allowed to perform this action
        в”Ӯ         on this specific resource?
        в”Ӯ         DENY вҶ’ Request blocked regardless of network
        в”Ӯ
        в””в”Җв”ҖвҶ’ Network Check (Network Plane)
                  Is traffic on this port to this destination
                  permitted by the security group?
                  DENY вҶ’ Request blocked regardless of IAM
```

Both must return **Allow** for the request to succeed. This defense-in-depth means a misconfigured IAM policy doesn't expose resources if network controls are correct, and vice versa.

---

## Summary: Responsibilities of Each Component

| Component | Responsibility | Key Security Property |
|---|---|---|
| **Instance Profile** | Binds IAM Role to EC2 instance | Eliminates hardcoded credentials |
| **IAM Role** | Defines the identity and permission scope | Temporary, auto-rotating credentials |
| **IAM Policy** | Enumerates specific allowed actions and resources | Least-privilege, resource-scoped |
| **Permission Boundaries** | Caps maximum permissions regardless of policy | Defense against privilege escalation |
| **EC2 Security Group** | Controls outbound traffic from the instance | Restricts which endpoints are reachable |
| **RDS Security Group** | Controls inbound traffic to the database | Allows only known, trusted sources |
| **VPC Endpoint (S3)** | Routes S3 traffic privately within AWS | Eliminates internet exposure |

> **Note:** The knowledge graph confirms the IAM roles, policy, security group, and VPC endpoint concepts used here. The specific EC2 instance profile binding mechanism and RDS IAM authentication details are standard AWS patterns inferred from these confirmed foundations. For authoritative implementation details, consult the AWS EC2 IAM Roles and RDS IAM Authentication documentation directly.

# Configuring CloudWatch Container Insights for ECS Task Monitoring & Memory Alarms

## Important Transparency Note

The underlying knowledge base contains **only high-level architectural information** about ECS/CloudWatch integration. The detailed configuration steps below are derived from general AWS knowledge, not from verified documentation in the provided context. I'll clearly distinguish what is confirmed vs. what requires external verification.

---

## Architecture Overview

```
ECS Cluster
    в””в”Җв”Җ ECS Service
            в””в”Җв”Җ ECS Task
                    в””в”Җв”Җ Container
                            в”ңв”Җв”Җ ECS Agent (lifecycle management)
                            в”ңв”Җв”Җ awslogs driver вҶ’ CloudWatch Log Group
                            в””в”Җв”Җ Container Insights вҶ’ CloudWatch Metrics вҶ’ Alarms
```

---

## Step 1: Enable Container Insights on the ECS Cluster

Container Insights must be enabled at the **cluster level** before any metrics are collected.

### AWS CLI
```bash
aws ecs update-cluster-settings \
  --cluster <your-cluster-name> \
  --settings name=containerInsights,value=enabled
```

### CloudFormation
```yaml
Type: AWS::ECS::Cluster
Properties:
  ClusterName: my-cluster
  ClusterSettings:
    - Name: containerInsights
      Value: enabled
```

> вҡ пёҸ **Verify:** Confirm this is the current enablement method in the [AWS Container Insights documentation](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonCloudWatch/latest/monitoring/ContainerInsights.html), as API behavior may change.

---

## Step 2: Configure the Task Execution Role (IAM)

The **Task Execution Role** must grant the ECS Agent permissions to publish metrics and logs to CloudWatch.

```json
{
  "Version": "2012-10-17",
  "Statement": [
    {
      "Effect": "Allow",
      "Action": [
        "logs:CreateLogGroup",
        "logs:CreateLogStream",
        "logs:PutLogEvents",
        "cloudwatch:PutMetricData"
      ],
      "Resource": "*"
    }
  ]
}
```

| Role Component | Purpose |
|----------------|---------|
| `logs:PutLogEvents` | Allows container stdout/stderr to reach CloudWatch Logs |
| `cloudwatch:PutMetricData` | Allows the ECS Agent to publish Container Insights metrics |

---

## Step 3: Configure the Task Definition

Two critical settings in the task definition affect memory monitoring:

```json
{
  "family": "my-task",
  "memory": "2048",
  "executionRoleArn": "arn:aws:iam::ACCOUNT_ID:role/TaskExecutionRole",
  "containerDefinitions": [
    {
      "name": "my-container",
      "image": "my-image:latest",
      "memory": 1024,
      "memoryReservation": 512,
      "logConfiguration": {
        "logDriver": "awslogs",
        "options": {
          "awslogs-group": "/ecs/my-task",
          "awslogs-region": "us-east-1",
          "awslogs-stream-prefix": "ecs"
        }
      }
    }
  ]
}
```

### Key Memory Parameters

| Parameter | Field | Behavior |
|-----------|-------|----------|
| Hard limit | `memory` | Container is killed if exceeded |
| Soft limit | `memoryReservation` | Reserved at scheduling; can burst above |
| Task-level limit | Top-level `memory` | Required for Fargate; caps total task memory |

---

## Step 4: Set CloudWatch Alarms on Memory Metrics

### Available Container Insights Memory Metrics

| Metric Name | Namespace | Description |
|-------------|-----------|-------------|
| `MemoryUtilized` | `ECS/ContainerInsights` | Actual MB used by the task |
| `MemoryReserved` | `ECS/ContainerInsights` | MB reserved in task definition |
| `MemoryUtilization` | `ECS/ContainerInsights` | Percentage of reserved memory used |

### Creating an Alarm via AWS CLI

```bash
aws cloudwatch put-metric-alarm \
  --alarm-name "ECS-High-Memory-Usage" \
  --metric-name MemoryUtilized \
  --namespace ECS/ContainerInsights \
  --dimensions \
      Name=ClusterName,Value=my-cluster \
      Name=ServiceName,Value=my-service \
  --statistic Average \
  --period 60 \
  --threshold 1800 \
  --comparison-operator GreaterThanThreshold \
  --evaluation-periods 3 \
  --alarm-actions arn:aws:sns:us-east-1:ACCOUNT_ID:my-alert-topic \
  --ok-actions arn:aws:sns:us-east-1:ACCOUNT_ID:my-alert-topic
```

### Alarm Parameter Explanation

| Parameter | Value | Rationale |
|-----------|-------|-----------|
| `--period 60` | 60 seconds | Granularity of metric evaluation |
| `--threshold 1800` | 1800 MB | ~88% of a 2048 MB task limit |
| `--evaluation-periods 3` | 3 consecutive periods | Reduces false positives from spikes |
| `--statistic Average` | Average | Smooths transient bursts |

---

## Step 5: Optional вҖ” Automated Scaling Response

You can connect alarms to automated actions via **EventBridge** or **Application Auto Scaling**:

```
CloudWatch Alarm (MemoryUtilized > threshold)
    в””в”Җв”Җ SNS Topic вҶ’ Alert notification
    в””в”Җв”Җ EventBridge Rule вҶ’ Lambda function (custom remediation)
    в””в”Җв”Җ Auto Scaling Policy вҶ’ Scale out ECS Service
```

### Auto Scaling Policy Example (CLI)
```bash
aws application-autoscaling put-scaling-policy \
  --service-namespace ecs \
  --scalable-dimension ecs:service:DesiredCount \
  --resource-id service/my-cluster/my-service \
  --policy-name memory-scale-out \
  --policy-type TargetTrackingScaling \
  --target-tracking-scaling-policy-configuration \
      '{"TargetValue": 75.0,
        "CustomizedMetricSpecification": {
          "MetricName": "MemoryUtilization",
          "Namespace": "ECS/ContainerInsights",
          "Dimensions": [
            {"Name": "ClusterName", "Value": "my-cluster"},
            {"Name": "ServiceName", "Value": "my-service"}
          ],
          "Statistic": "Average"
        }}'
```

---

## Complete Dependency Summary

```
1. ECS Cluster
   в””в”Җв”Җ Container Insights: ENABLED
           вҶ“
2. Task Execution Role (IAM)
   в””в”Җв”Җ Permissions: logs:*, cloudwatch:PutMetricData
           вҶ“
3. Task Definition
   в”ңв”Җв”Җ memory / memoryReservation (hard/soft limits)
   в””в”Җв”Җ logConfiguration (awslogs driver вҶ’ Log Group)
           вҶ“
4. CloudWatch
   в”ңв”Җв”Җ Namespace: ECS/ContainerInsights
   в”ңв”Җв”Җ Metrics: MemoryUtilized, MemoryReserved, MemoryUtilization
   в””в”Җв”Җ Alarms вҶ’ SNS / Auto Scaling / EventBridge
```

---

## вҡ пёҸ Limitations of This Answer

| Area | Confidence | Recommendation |
|------|------------|----------------|
| Cluster-level enablement | High | Verify CLI syntax in AWS docs |
| Metric names/namespace | Medium | Confirm in [CloudWatch Container Insights metrics reference](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonCloudWatch/latest/monitoring/Container-Insights-metrics-ECS.html) |
| IAM permissions | High | Test with least-privilege in your environment |
| Auto Scaling integration | Medium | Consult Application Auto Scaling documentation |

The knowledge base confirms CloudWatch integrates with ECS for **logs, metrics, and Container Insights**, but does not contain the operational configuration details вҖ” those steps above should be validated against current AWS documentation before production use.

# Reliable Asynchronous Lambda Processing: Integrated Architecture

## How the Three Components Work Together

These three mechanisms operate as **complementary layers** in a defense-in-depth reliability strategy, each addressing a different phase of failure handling:

---

## Layer 1: Lambda Function Destinations (Primary Outcome Routing)

Lambda Destinations are the **first line of structured response** to both success and failure:

```json
{
  "EventSourceMappingConfiguration": {
    "DestinationConfig": {
      "OnSuccess": {
        "Destination": "arn:aws:sqs:us-east-1:123456789:success-queue"
      },
      "OnFailure": {
        "Destination": "arn:aws:sqs:us-east-1:123456789:failure-queue"
      }
    }
  }
}
```

**Key characteristics:**
| Feature | Destinations | DLQ |
|---|---|---|
| Captures successes | вң… Yes | вқҢ No |
| Captures failures | вң… Yes | вң… Yes |
| Includes execution metadata | вң… Rich context | вқҢ Payload only |
| Target options | SQS, SNS, EventBridge, Lambda | SQS, SNS only |
| Trigger timing | After retries exhausted | After retries exhausted |

> **Critical distinction:** Destinations provide the **full execution context** (request ID, error details, stack trace, retry attempts), making downstream debugging and automated remediation significantly more actionable than raw DLQ payloads.

---

## Layer 2: Dead-Letter Queue (Safety Net Fallback)

The DLQ serves as a **durable fallback** when Destinations themselves fail or are not configured:

```
Async Invocation
      в”Ӯ
      в”ңв”Җв–ә Retry 1 в”Җв”Җв–ә FAIL
      в”ңв”Җв–ә Retry 2 в”Җв”Җв–ә FAIL
      в”Ӯ
      в–ј
Destination OnFailure в”Җв”Җв–ә [Destination unreachable?]
      в”Ӯ                              в”Ӯ
      в–ј                              в–ј
Failure routed                  DLQ catches
to destination                  the event
```

**DLQ configuration on the function:**
```json
{
  "DeadLetterConfig": {
    "TargetArn": "arn:aws:sqs:us-east-1:123456789:my-dlq"
  }
}
```

**Required IAM permissions** via `AWSLambdaSQSQueueExecutionRole`:
- `sqs:ReceiveMessage`
- `sqs:DeleteMessage`
- `sqs:GetQueueAttributes`

**When to use DLQ alongside Destinations:**
- As a **redundant capture mechanism** if the Destination target is unavailable
- For **compliance requirements** mandating durable event preservation
- When integrating with **legacy systems** that poll SQS directly

---

## Layer 3: CloudWatch Alarms (Proactive Observability)

CloudWatch operates **across both layers above**, providing visibility and automated response:

### Critical Metrics to Monitor

| Metric | Source | Alarm Threshold | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| `Errors` | Lambda namespace | > 0 for N minutes | Function failures driving retries |
| `DeadLetterErrors` | Lambda namespace | > 0 | Lambda **cannot write** to DLQ вҖ” silent failure risk |
| `DestinationDeliveryFailures` | Lambda namespace | > 0 | Destination routing failed |
| `ApproximateNumberOfMessagesVisible` | SQS namespace | > threshold | DLQ accumulating unprocessed failures |
| `Throttles` | Lambda namespace | > 0 | Capacity issues causing async backpressure |

### Alarm Action Chain

```
CloudWatch Alarm (DLQ depth > 10)
        в”Ӯ
        в–ј
SNS Topic в”Җв”Җв–ә PagerDuty/Email (human escalation)
        в”Ӯ
        в–ј
Remediation Lambda в”Җв”Җв–ә Reprocess DLQ messages
        в”Ӯ
        в–ј
EventBridge Rule в”Җв”Җв–ә Trigger incident workflow
```

> вҡ пёҸ **Critical gap to monitor:** `DeadLetterErrors` indicates Lambda **failed to deliver to the DLQ itself** вҖ” meaning events are being silently dropped. This alarm is frequently overlooked but represents total failure of the safety net.

---

## Complete Integrated Architecture

```
EventBridge / S3 / SNS
        в”Ӯ
        в–ј (async invocation)
в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
в”Ӯ   Lambda Function       в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ   - Retry 1             в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ   - Retry 2             в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
        в”Ӯ
        в”ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ SUCCESS в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв–ә Destination (OnSuccess)
        в”Ӯ                                    в”Ӯ
        в”Ӯ                             SQS/SNS/EventBridge
        в”Ӯ                             (downstream processing)
        в”Ӯ
        в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ FAILURE (retries exhausted)
                    в”Ӯ
                    в”ңв”Җв”Җв–ә Destination (OnFailure) в—„в”Җв”Җ Rich metadata
                    в”Ӯ           в”Ӯ
                    в”Ӯ    [If destination fails]
                    в”Ӯ           в”Ӯ
                    в””в”Җв”Җв–ә DLQ (SQS) в—„в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ AWSLambdaSQSQueueExecutionRole
                              в”Ӯ
                              в–ј
                    CloudWatch Metrics
                              в”Ӯ
                    в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
                    в–ј                    в–ј
              Alarm: DLQ depth    Alarm: DeadLetterErrors
                    в”Ӯ                    в”Ӯ
                    в–ј                    в–ј
              SNS Alert          CRITICAL: Silent
              + Auto-remediation  drop alert вҶ’ page
```

---

## Recommended Implementation Pattern

### Step 1: Configure Destinations first (primary routing)
```python
lambda_client.put_function_event_invoke_config(
    FunctionName='my-function',
    MaximumRetryAttempts=2,
    DestinationConfig={
        'OnSuccess': {'Destination': success_queue_arn},
        'OnFailure': {'Destination': failure_queue_arn}
    }
)
```

### Step 2: Add DLQ as redundant safety net
```python
lambda_client.update_function_configuration(
    FunctionName='my-function',
    DeadLetterConfig={'TargetArn': dlq_arn}
)
```

### Step 3: Create CloudWatch alarms for all failure modes
```python
cloudwatch.put_metric_alarm(
    AlarmName='Lambda-DLQ-Depth',
    MetricName='ApproximateNumberOfMessagesVisible',
    Namespace='AWS/SQS',
    Threshold=1,
    AlarmActions=[sns_topic_arn]
)
```

---

## Summary: Responsibility Matrix

| Scenario | Handled By |
|---|---|
| Function succeeds | Destination (OnSuccess) |
| Function fails after retries | Destination (OnFailure) |
| Destination unreachable | DLQ catches the event |
| DLQ write fails | CloudWatch `DeadLetterErrors` alarm |
| DLQ accumulating | CloudWatch SQS depth alarm |
| Capacity/throttle issues | CloudWatch `Throttles` alarm |
| Silent drops (all layers fail) | CloudWatch `DestinationDeliveryFailures` |

The three components are **not redundant** вҖ” they are **sequential layers** where each catches what the previous layer cannot, with CloudWatch providing the cross-cutting observability that makes the entire chain auditable and actionable.

# Building an Event-Driven Pipeline with S3 and Lambda

## Architecture Overview

```
File Upload вҶ’ S3 Bucket вҶ’ Object Event вҶ’ Lambda Trigger вҶ’ File Processing
```

This pipeline relies on **asynchronous invocation**: S3 fires the event and does **not wait** for Lambda's response вҖ” Lambda processes the file independently in the background.

---

## Core Components

### 1. S3 Object Events
S3 emits structured events when objects are created, deleted, or modified. For file upload pipelines, the relevant event types are:

| Event Type | When It Fires |
|---|---|
| `s3:ObjectCreated:Put` | Direct file upload |
| `s3:ObjectCreated:Post` | HTML form upload |
| `s3:ObjectCreated:CompleteMultipartUpload` | Large file upload |
| `s3:ObjectCreated:*` | Any of the above |

### 2. Lambda Event Sources
S3 is registered as a Lambda **event source/trigger**. When S3 emits an object event, it passes a structured JSON payload to Lambda:

```json
{
  "Records": [{
    "eventName": "ObjectCreated:Put",
    "s3": {
      "bucket": { "name": "my-bucket" },
      "object": { "key": "uploads/file.csv", "size": 1024 }
    }
  }]
}
```

---

## Step-by-Step Setup

### Step 1: Configure S3 Bucket Notification
Tell S3 which Lambda function to invoke and for which events:

```json
{
  "LambdaFunctionConfigurations": [{
    "LambdaFunctionArn": "arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789:function:ProcessFile",
    "Events": ["s3:ObjectCreated:*"],
    "Filter": {
      "Key": {
        "FilterRules": [{ "Name": "suffix", "Value": ".csv" }]
      }
    }
  }]
}
```
> The `Filter` block is optional but recommended вҖ” it limits triggers to only `.csv` files, reducing unnecessary invocations.

### Step 2: Grant S3 Permission to Invoke Lambda
S3 needs explicit permission to call your Lambda function:

```bash
aws lambda add-permission \
  --function-name ProcessFile \
  --principal s3.amazonaws.com \
  --action lambda:InvokeFunction \
  --source-arn arn:aws:s3:::my-bucket
```

### Step 3: Write the Lambda Handler
Parse the S3 event and process the uploaded file:

```python
import boto3

def handler(event, context):
    s3 = boto3.client('s3')
    
    for record in event['Records']:
        bucket = record['s3']['bucket']['name']
        key    = record['s3']['object']['key']
        
        # Retrieve the uploaded file
        obj  = s3.get_object(Bucket=bucket, Key=key)
        data = obj['Body'].read()
        
        # Your processing logic here
        process_file(data)
```

---

## Invocation Behavior & Reliability

Because S3вҶ’Lambda is **asynchronous**, you must account for failure scenarios:

| Concern | Recommendation |
|---|---|
| **Failed invocations** | Configure a **Dead Letter Queue (DLQ)** or Lambda Destinations to capture errors |
| **Automatic retries** | Lambda retries up to **2 times** by default on failure |
| **Duplicate events** | Make your handler **idempotent** (safe to run multiple times on the same file) |
| **Large files** | Avoid loading entire files into memory; use streaming or S3 presigned URLs |
| **Timeout** | Default Lambda timeout is 3 seconds вҖ” **increase it** for file processing workloads |
| **Concurrency spikes** | Set **reserved concurrency** to protect downstream systems |

---

## Extending to a Multi-Target Pipeline

For more complex workflows, route S3 events through **EventBridge** to fan out to multiple services:

```
S3 вҶ’ EventBridge вҶ’ Lambda A (validate)
                 вҶ’ Lambda B (transform)
                 вҶ’ Lambda C (notify)
                 вҶ’ SQS / SNS / Step Functions
```

This decouples your pipeline stages and allows independent scaling and filtering of each target.

---

## Key Takeaways

1. **S3 object events** (`s3:ObjectCreated:*`) are the trigger mechanism
2. **Asynchronous invocation** means S3 doesn't wait вҖ” design for eventual processing
3. **Permissions** must be explicitly granted for S3 to invoke Lambda
4. **Idempotency + DLQ** are essential for production reliability
5. **EventBridge** enables fan-out to multiple downstream processors when needed

# EKS OIDC + IRSA + Kubernetes Service Accounts: Complete Architecture

> **Transparency Note:** The retrieved context does not contain EKS, OIDC, IRSA, or Kubernetes-specific nodes. The answer below is based on general AWS knowledge. I'll clearly indicate where graph context applies vs. general knowledge.

---

## Conceptual Overview

The core problem IRSA solves: **How do you give a specific pod AWS permissions without hardcoding credentials or over-permissioning an entire node?**

## The Three Components in Detail

### Component 1: EKS OIDC Provider

**What it is:** Every EKS cluster automatically exposes an OpenID Connect endpoint. You register this endpoint as a trusted Identity Provider in IAM вҖ” this is a one-time setup per cluster.

```bash
# Get your cluster's OIDC issuer URL
aws eks describe-cluster \
  --name my-cluster \
  --query "cluster.identity.oidc.issuer" \
  --output text
# Returns: https://oidc.eks.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/id/EXAMPLED539D4633E53DE1B71EXAMPLE

# Register it as an IAM Identity Provider
eksctl utils associate-iam-oidc-provider \
  --cluster my-cluster \
  --approve
```

**What it does:**
- Publishes a **discovery document** at `/.well-known/openid-configuration`
- Publishes **public signing keys** (JWKS) that IAM uses to verify JWT signatures
- Issues **signed JWT tokens** for each Kubernetes service account
- The issuer URL is **globally unique per cluster** вҖ” this is the security boundary at the cluster level

**The JWT token payload looks like:**
```json
{
  "iss": "https://oidc.eks.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/id/CLUSTER_ID",
  "sub": "system:serviceaccount:my-namespace:my-service-account",
  "aud": ["sts.amazonaws.com"],
  "exp": 1234567890,
  "iat": 1234567890,
  "kubernetes.io": {
    "namespace": "my-namespace",
    "serviceaccount": {
      "name": "my-service-account",
      "uid": "abc123"
    },
    "pod": {
      "name": "my-pod-xyz",
      "uid": "def456"
    }
  }
}
```

The `sub` claim (`system:serviceaccount:NAMESPACE:NAME`) is the critical field IAM uses for scoping.

---

### Component 2: IAM Role with IRSA Trust Policy

**What it is:** A standard IAM role, but with a trust policy that specifically allows the OIDC provider to assume it вҖ” and only for a specific Kubernetes namespace and service account.

```json
{
  "Version": "2012-10-17",
  "Statement": [
    {
      "Effect": "Allow",
      "Principal": {
        "Federated": "arn:aws:iam::123456789012:oidc-provider/oidc.eks.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/id/CLUSTER_ID"
      },
      "Action": "sts:AssumeRoleWithWebIdentity",
      "Condition": {
        "StringEquals": {
          "oidc.eks.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/id/CLUSTER_ID:sub":
            "system:serviceaccount:my-namespace:my-service-account",
          "oidc.eks.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/id/CLUSTER_ID:aud":
            "sts.amazonaws.com"
        }
      }
    }
  ]
}
```

**Key design decisions in this trust policy:**

| Field | Purpose | Security Value |
|-------|---------|----------------|
| `Principal.Federated` | Trusts only this specific cluster's OIDC provider | Prevents cross-cluster token reuse |
| `Condition:sub` | Scopes to exact namespace + service account | Prevents other pods from assuming the role |
| `Condition:aud` | Ensures token was meant for STS | Prevents token reuse from other audiences |

**The permission policy** attached to this role is standard IAM вҖ” it defines what AWS actions the pod can actually perform:

```json
{
  "Version": "2012-10-17",
  "Statement": [
    {
      "Effect": "Allow",
      "Action": [
        "s3:GetObject",
        "s3:PutObject"
      ],
      "Resource": "arn:aws:s3:::my-app-bucket/*"
    }
  ]
}
```

---

### Component 3: Kubernetes Service Account

**What it is:** A Kubernetes-native identity object that pods run as. When annotated with an IAM Role ARN, the **EKS Pod Identity Webhook** (a mutating admission webhook) automatically injects credentials infrastructure into pods.

```yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: ServiceAccount
metadata:
  name: my-service-account
  namespace: my-namespace
  annotations:
    eks.amazonaws.com/role-arn: arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/my-irsa-role
    # Optional: customize token expiry (default 86400s / 24h)
    eks.amazonaws.com/token-expiration: "3600"
```

**What the webhook injects into pods using this ServiceAccount:**

```yaml
# These are automatically added to your pod spec вҖ” you don't write them
spec:
  containers:
  - name: my-app
    env:
    - name: AWS_ROLE_ARN
      value: "arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/my-irsa-role"
    - name: AWS_WEB_IDENTITY_TOKEN_FILE
      value: "/var/run/secrets/eks.amazonaws.com/serviceaccount/token"
    volumeMounts:
    - name: aws-iam-token
      mountPath: /var/run/secrets/eks.amazonaws.com/serviceaccount
      readOnly: true
  volumes:
  - name: aws-iam-token
    projected:
      sources:
      - serviceAccountToken:
          audience: sts.amazonaws.com
          expirationSeconds: 86400
          path: token
```

The token is a **projected service account token** вҖ” not the default Kubernetes service account token. It has:
- A specific audience (`sts.amazonaws.com`)
- A bounded expiry
- Automatic rotation by kubelet before expiry

---

## The Complete Credential Flow (Step by Step)

```
Time в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв–¶

[Setup Phase - done once]
1. Register EKS OIDC endpoint as IAM Identity Provider
2. Create IAM Role with trust policy referencing OIDC provider
3. Annotate Kubernetes ServiceAccount with IAM Role ARN

[Runtime Phase - every pod startup]
4. Pod scheduled, references annotated ServiceAccount
5. Admission webhook intercepts pod creation
6. Webhook injects: env vars + projected token volume mount
7. Pod starts with JWT token at known file path

[AWS API Call Phase - every time pod calls AWS]
8. App code calls AWS SDK (e.g., s3.GetObject())
9. SDK credential provider chain detects AWS_WEB_IDENTITY_TOKEN_FILE
10. SDK reads JWT from mounted file path
11. SDK calls sts:AssumeRoleWithWebIdentity with JWT
12. STS fetches OIDC public keys from cluster's JWKS endpoint
13. STS validates JWT signature and claims (sub, aud, exp)
14. STS returns temporary credentials (15min - 12hr lifetime)
15. SDK caches credentials, retries before expiry
16. AWS API call proceeds with temporary credentials
```

---

## Common Patterns and Gotchas

### Pattern: One Role Per Workload (Recommended)
```
my-namespace/api-service-account      вҶ’ arn:aws:iam::ACCT:role/api-role
my-namespace/worker-service-account   вҶ’ arn:aws:iam::ACCT:role/worker-role
my-namespace/cronjob-service-account  вҶ’ arn:aws:iam::ACCT:role/cronjob-role
```
Each workload gets least-privilege permissions independently.

### Pattern: Cross-Account Access
```json
// Role in Account B trusts OIDC provider from Account A's EKS cluster
{
  "Principal": {
    "Federated": "arn:aws:iam::ACCOUNT_A:oidc-provider/oidc.eks..."
  }
}
```

### Common Gotchas

| Problem | Cause | Fix |
|---------|-------|-----|
| `AccessDenied` on `AssumeRoleWithWebIdentity` | Trust policy `sub` condition doesn't match | Verify namespace and SA name exactly match |
| Credentials not injected | Pod created before webhook was installed | Restart pod after webhook setup |
| Token expired errors | Token expiry too short for long-running jobs | Increase `eks.amazonaws.com/token-expiration` annotation |
| Works locally, fails in cluster | Local uses `~/.aws/credentials`, cluster uses IRSA | Ensure SDK version supports web identity token provider |
| Role assumed but wrong permissions | Permission policy too restrictive | Check CloudTrail for the actual API calls being denied |

---

## Comparison: IRSA vs. Other Approaches

| Approach | Credential Scope | Rotation | Least Privilege |
|----------|-----------------|----------|-----------------|
| **IRSA** | Per pod/workload | Automatic | вң… Full |
| Node IAM Role | All pods on node | Automatic | вқҢ Shared |
| Hardcoded secrets | Per secret | Manual | вң… But risky |
| Secrets Manager injection | Per pod | Configurable | вң… But complex |
| **EKS Pod Identity** (newer) | Per pod | Automatic | вң… Full (simpler setup) |

> **Note:** AWS released **EKS Pod Identity** in 2023 as a simpler alternative to IRSA that doesn't require managing OIDC providers manually. IRSA remains widely used and fully supported.

---

For complete coverage of this topic, the knowledge graph would benefit from these additional nodes and relationships:

```
aws.eks в”Җв”Җhasв”Җв”Җв–¶ aws.eks.oidc_provider в”Җв”Җregisters_withв”Җв”Җв–¶ aws.iam.identity_providers

```

The existing `aws.iam.roles`, `aws.sts`, and `aws.iam.policies` nodes are the correct foundation вҖ” they just need EKS and Kubernetes nodes connected to them.

# Configuring ALB for HTTPS Termination and HTTP-to-HTTPS Redirect

> **Note on sources:** The retrieved context explicitly states it lacks detailed ALB listener/HTTPS configuration nodes. The answer below is based on established AWS ALB architecture patterns rather than graph-specific documentation. Treat this as general AWS guidance.

---

## Core Concept

ALB handles TLS at the load balancer layer, meaning:
- **Port 443** вҶ’ ALB decrypts TLS, forwards plain HTTP to targets
- **Port 80** вҶ’ ALB issues a redirect response (never touches backend)

```
Client HTTPS :443  вҶ’  ALB (terminates TLS)  вҶ’  Target :8080 (plain HTTP)
Client HTTP  :80   вҶ’  ALB (301 redirect)    вҶ’  Client redirected to HTTPS
```

---

## Step-by-Step Configuration

### Step 1: Obtain an ACM Certificate

```hcl
# Request a certificate (DNS validation recommended)
resource "aws_acm_certificate" "cert" {
  domain_name               = "example.com"
  subject_alternative_names = ["*.example.com"]
  validation_method         = "DNS"

  lifecycle {
    create_before_destroy = true
  }
}

# Validate via Route53
resource "aws_acm_certificate_validation" "cert" {
  certificate_arn         = aws_acm_certificate.cert.arn
  validation_record_fqdns = [for record in aws_route53_record.cert_validation : record.fqdn]
}
```

> вҡ пёҸ The certificate **must be in the same AWS region** as the ALB. CloudFront requires `us-east-1`.

---

### Step 2: Create the ALB

```hcl
resource "aws_lb" "main" {
  name               = "app-alb"
  internal           = false
  load_balancer_type = "application"
  security_groups    = [aws_security_group.alb_sg.id]
  subnets            = var.public_subnet_ids

  enable_deletion_protection = true

  access_logs {
    bucket  = aws_s3_bucket.alb_logs.bucket
    enabled = true
  }
}
```

---

### Step 3: HTTPS Listener (Port 443) вҖ” TLS Termination

```hcl
resource "aws_lb_listener" "https" {
  load_balancer_arn = aws_lb.main.arn
  port              = "443"
  protocol          = "HTTPS"

  # TLS policy вҖ” prefer TLS 1.3/1.2 only
  ssl_policy        = "ELBSecurityPolicy-TLS13-1-2-2021-06"

  # ACM certificate for TLS termination
  certificate_arn   = aws_acm_certificate_validation.cert.certificate_arn

  default_action {
    type             = "forward"
    target_group_arn = aws_lb_target_group.app.arn
  }
}

# Optional: attach additional certificates (SNI)
resource "aws_lb_listener_certificate" "additional" {
  listener_arn    = aws_lb_listener.https.arn
  certificate_arn = aws_acm_certificate.secondary.arn
}
```

**SSL Policy Comparison:**

| Policy | TLS Versions | Use Case |
|--------|-------------|----------|
| `ELBSecurityPolicy-TLS13-1-2-2021-06` | 1.2, 1.3 | вң… Recommended default |
| `ELBSecurityPolicy-TLS13-1-3-2021-06` | 1.3 only | Maximum security |
| `ELBSecurityPolicy-2016-08` | 1.0вҖ“1.2 | Legacy compatibility only |

---

### Step 4: HTTP Listener (Port 80) вҖ” Redirect to HTTPS

```hcl
resource "aws_lb_listener" "http_redirect" {
  load_balancer_arn = aws_lb.main.arn
  port              = "80"
  protocol          = "HTTP"

  default_action {
    type = "redirect"

    redirect {
      port        = "443"
      protocol    = "HTTPS"
      status_code = "HTTP_301"   # Permanent вҖ” use HTTP_302 during testing
    }
  }
}
```

> **301 vs 302:** Use `HTTP_301` (permanent) in production so browsers cache the redirect. Use `HTTP_302` (temporary) while testing to avoid browser caching issues.

---

### Step 5: Target Group Configuration

```hcl
resource "aws_lb_target_group" "app" {
  name        = "app-targets"
  port        = 8080          # Backend receives plain HTTP
  protocol    = "HTTP"        # NOT HTTPS вҖ” ALB already terminated TLS
  vpc_id      = var.vpc_id
  target_type = "ip"          # Use "ip" for ECS Fargate, "instance" for EC2

  health_check {
    enabled             = true
    path                = "/health"
    protocol            = "HTTP"
    healthy_threshold   = 2
    unhealthy_threshold = 3
    timeout             = 5
    interval            = 30
    matcher             = "200"
  }

  deregistration_delay = 30
}
```

---

### Step 6: Security Groups

```hcl
# ALB Security Group вҖ” public-facing
resource "aws_security_group" "alb_sg" {
  name   = "alb-sg"
  vpc_id = var.vpc_id

  ingress {
    description = "HTTP for redirect"
    from_port   = 80
    to_port     = 80
    protocol    = "tcp"
    cidr_blocks = ["0.0.0.0/0"]
    ipv6_cidr_blocks = ["::/0"]
  }

  ingress {
    description = "HTTPS"
    from_port   = 443
    to_port     = 443
    protocol    = "tcp"
    cidr_blocks = ["0.0.0.0/0"]
    ipv6_cidr_blocks = ["::/0"]
  }

  egress {
    from_port   = 0
    to_port     = 0
    protocol    = "-1"
    cidr_blocks = ["0.0.0.0/0"]
  }
}

# Application/ECS Security Group вҖ” only accepts traffic FROM ALB
resource "aws_security_group" "app_sg" {
  name   = "app-sg"
  vpc_id = var.vpc_id

  ingress {
    description     = "From ALB only"
    from_port       = 8080
    to_port         = 8080
    protocol        = "tcp"
    security_groups = [aws_security_group.alb_sg.id]  # Restrict to ALB SG
  }

  egress {
    from_port   = 0
    to_port     = 0
    protocol    = "-1"
    cidr_blocks = ["0.0.0.0/0"]
  }
}
```

---

## Advanced: Listener Rules for Path/Host Routing

```hcl
# Route /api/* to a different target group
resource "aws_lb_listener_rule" "api_routing" {
  listener_arn = aws_lb_listener.https.arn
  priority     = 100

  action {
    type             = "forward"
    target_group_arn = aws_lb_target_group.api.arn
  }

  condition {
    path_pattern {
      values = ["/api/*"]
    }
  }
}

# Host-based routing for multiple domains
resource "aws_lb_listener_rule" "host_routing" {
  listener_arn = aws_lb_listener.https.arn
  priority     = 200

  action {
    type             = "forward"
    target_group_arn = aws_lb_target_group.secondary_app.arn
  }

  condition {
    host_header {
      values = ["api.example.com"]
    }
  }
}
```

## Key Checklist

- [ ] ACM certificate validated and in the **same region** as ALB
- [ ] Port **80 and 443** open in ALB security group
- [ ] App security group restricts inbound to **ALB security group only**
- [ ] Target group protocol is **HTTP** (not HTTPS) вҖ” ALB handles TLS
- [ ] SSL policy uses **TLS 1.2 minimum** (`TLS13-1-2-2021-06`)
- [ ] Use `HTTP_301` for permanent redirect in production
- [ ] Health check path returns **HTTP 200** from backend
